disease.name: Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma
disease.note: Most common pancreatic malignancy — ductal adenocarcinoma. Presents with painless jaundice (head), epigastric pain radiating to back (body/tail), weight loss, and new-onset diabetes. Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder in jaundice) is classic but late. Overall 5-year survival is <10%. Only 15-20% are resectable at presentation.
presentingComplaints: Yellow discoloration of eyes and skin without pain
Pain in the upper abdomen radiating to the back
New onset of diabetes or difficulty controlling diabetes
pathophysiologyDetail: Pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) is the precursor lesion: PanIN-1 (flat, mucinous columnar epithelium without atypia) → PanIN-2 (nuclear abnormalities, pseudostratification) → PanIN-3/carcinoma in situ (severe atypia, cribriforming, budding) → invasive adenocarcinoma; progression takes 10-15 years from initiating mutation to invasive cancer

KRAS mutation is the earliest and most frequent genetic event (>90% of pancreatic cancers): codon 12 point mutation (G12D most common) → constitutive activation of KRAS → downstream signaling via RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT pathways → uncontrolled cell proliferation; KRAS mutation is present in PanIN-1 lesions as the initiating event

CDKN2A/p16 inactivation (80-95%): tumor suppressor gene encoding p16 protein → loss of cell cycle regulation at G1/S checkpoint → allows unregulated proliferation; p16 loss occurs at PanIN-2 stage; germline CDKN2A mutations cause Familial Atypical Multiple Mole Melanoma (FAMMM) syndrome with high pancreatic cancer risk

TP53 mutation (50-75%): late event at PanIN-3 stage → loss of p53 tumor suppressor protein → loss of DNA damage response, apoptosis, and cell cycle arrest → genomic instability → chromosomal rearrangements, copy number alterations, chromothripsis

SMAD4/DPC4 inactivation (55%): loss of SMAD4 leads to disrupted TGF-β signaling → loss of growth inhibition → more aggressive phenotype with widespread metastasis; SMAD4 loss is associated with poorer prognosis and higher metastatic potential; retained SMAD4 correlates with locally advanced, less metastatic disease

Desmoplastic stromal reaction is the hallmark of pancreatic cancer: activated pancreatic stellate cells (PSCs) → extensive collagen deposition, fibronectin, hyaluronan → dense desmoplastic stroma that compresses blood vessels → hypovascular tumor → chemotherapy drug delivery impaired; PSCs also secrete growth factors (HGF, FGF, PDGF) that promote tumor progression

Perineural invasion is a characteristic feature (80-100% of resected specimens): tumor cells invade along nerve sheaths within the perineural space → intractable back pain (classic presenting symptom) → retrograde spread along celiac, splanchnic, and mesenteric nerves → contributes to local recurrence after resection even with negative margins

Lymphatic metastasis: primary drainage to peripancreatic lymph nodes (superior and inferior pancreaticoduodenal, pyloric, celiac, SMA, para-aortic) → involved in 50-70% at diagnosis; lymph node ratio (>0.2) is the strongest prognostic factor after margin status; skip metastases occur in 10-15%

Hepatic metastasis is the most common distant site: portal venous drainage carries tumor cells directly to liver; liver micrometastases present at diagnosis in 50-70% even with 'resectable' disease by conventional imaging; explains high recurrence rate after curative-intent resection

Immunological microenvironment: pancreatic cancer is an immunologically 'cold' tumor — low T cell infiltration, high myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs - M2 polarized), regulatory T cells (Tregs) → potent immunosuppression; explains poor response to checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy; TGF-β in stroma excludes T cells from tumor

Epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT): tumor cells lose epithelial markers (E-cadherin) and gain mesenchymal markers (vimentin, N-cadherin, Snail, Twist) → increased migratory and invasive capacity → dissemination; EMT is driven by KRAS signaling, TGF-β, and hypoxia; contributes to chemoresistance

Metabolic reprogramming: pancreatic cancer exhibits Warburg effect — aerobic glycolysis even in the presence of oxygen; increased glucose uptake (basis of FDG-PET); dependence on glutamine metabolism (via KRAS-driven pathway); autophagy provides nutrients in nutrient-poor environment; macropinocytosis of extracellular proteins as amino acid source

East African context: pancreatic cancer is less common than in Western populations but incidence is rising with dietary changes, obesity, and smoking; aflatoxin exposure (maize, groundnuts) may contribute through co-carcinogenesis; late presentation with advanced unresectable disease is the norm; access to pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) limited to 2-3 centers in Kenya; chemotherapy options limited by cost

Hereditary risk factors in East African context: BRCA2 mutations in specific populations (e.g., Afrikaner, Ashkenazi); hereditary pancreatitis (PRSS1 mutations) rare but reported; Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11) with high lifetime risk (30-60%); family history often under-reported; genetic testing rarely available; PALB2, ATM, and Lynch syndrome also increase risk

landmarkTrials.trial: FOLFIRINOX vs Gemcitabine for Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer (2011, NEJM — PRODIGE 4/ACCORD 11)
landmarkTrials.population: 342 patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer, ECOG 0-1
landmarkTrials.intervention: FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin, irinotecan, 5-FU, leucovorin) vs Gemcitabine monotherapy
landmarkTrials.outcome: FOLFIRINOX: median OS 11.1 vs 6.8 months (p<0.001); median PFS 6.4 vs 3.3 months; response rate 31.6% vs 9.4%. Greater toxicity (neutropenia 45.7%, febrile neutropenia 5.4%, diarrhea, neuropathy). FOLFIRINOX became the standard for fit patients (ECOG 0-1) with metastatic disease.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: FOLFIRINOX is expensive and toxic; rarely used in Kenya due to cost and limited ICU support; Gemcitabine remains more accessible

landmarkTrials.trial: Gemcitabine + Nab-paclitaxel vs Gemcitabine for Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer (2013, NEJM — MPACT)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Gem + Nab-paclitaxel: median OS 8.5 vs 6.7 months (p<0.001); median PFS 5.5 vs 3.7 months; response rate 23% vs 7%. More neuropathy (17%), neutropenia, and fatigue. A second option for metastatic disease, with better tolerability than FOLFIRINOX.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Nab-paclitaxel is expensive but better tolerated than FOLFIRINOX; limited availability in Kenya

landmarkTrials.trial: Adjuvant Gemcitabine vs Observation for Resected Pancreatic Cancer (2004, NEJM — CONKO-001)
landmarkTrials.population: 368 patients with R0/R1 resected pancreatic cancer
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adjuvant Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² weekly × 3 every 4 weeks × 6 cycles vs observation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Adjuvant Gemcitabine: median DFS 13.4 vs 6.9 months (p<0.001); median OS 22.1 vs 20.2 months (longer follow-up showed 5-year OS 20.7% vs 10.4%). Established adjuvant chemotherapy as standard after resection.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Adjuvant Gemcitabine is the most accessible option in Kenya after Whipple resection

landmarkTrials.trial: Adjuvant mFOLFIRINOX vs Gemcitabine for Resected Pancreatic Cancer (2018, NEJM — PRODIGE 24/CCTG PA.6)
landmarkTrials.population: 493 patients with resected pancreatic cancer, younger than 79, ECOG 0-1
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adjuvant mFOLFIRINOX (modified dose) for 6 months vs Gemcitabine for 6 months
landmarkTrials.outcome: mFOLFIRINOX: median DFS 21.6 vs 12.8 months (p<0.001); median OS 54.4 vs 35.0 months (p<0.001); 3-year OS 63.4% vs 48.6%. More toxicity (diarrhea 19%, mucositis 10%, neutropenia 47%). New standard for fit patients after resection.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: mFOLFIRINOX limited to well-resourced patients in Kenya due to cost and toxicity management

landmarkTrials.trial: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX vs Upfront Surgery for Borderline Resectable (2020, JAMA Oncology — SWOG S1505)
landmarkTrials.population: 104 patients with borderline resectable pancreatic cancer
landmarkTrials.intervention: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX × 2 months → surgery → adjuvant FOLFIRINOX vs upfront surgery → adjuvant FOLFIRINOX
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in OS (29.8 vs 27.0 months, p=0.79). Both arms had high resection rates (73% vs 79%). Neoadjuvant approach did not improve R0 rate or survival in this trial. Nodal status and CA 19-9 response more prognostic than timing.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Upfront surgery remains an option for resectable disease in Kenya when affordable; neoadjuvant for borderline cases

landmarkTrials.trial: ESPAC-4: Adjuvant Gemcitabine + Capecitabine vs Gemcitabine (2017, The Lancet)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Gemcitabine + Capecitabine (oral) vs Gemcitabine alone × 6 months
landmarkTrials.outcome: Gem + Capecitabine: median OS 28.0 vs 25.5 months (p=0.032); 5-year survival 28.8% vs 16.3%. Combination improved survival with manageable toxicity. Alternative to mFOLFIRINOX when FOLFIRINOX unsuitable.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Gemcitabine + Capecitabine is accessible in Kenya; oral Capecitabine avoids IV infusion costs

landmarkTrials.trial: Palliative Chemotherapy for Advanced Pancreatic Cancer — Meta-analysis (2006, Cochrane)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 51 RCTs for advanced pancreatic cancer
landmarkTrials.intervention: Various chemotherapy regimens vs best supportive care
landmarkTrials.outcome: Chemotherapy improved median OS from 3-4 months to 6-10 months over best supportive care (HR 0.64). 5-FU-based regimens offered modest benefit. Gemcitabine improved clinical benefit response (pain, performance status, weight) by 23.8% vs 4.8% for 5-FU. Platinum combinations offered modest additional benefit.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Palliative chemotherapy is accessible in Kenya for eligible patients; Gemcitabine is standard first-line

landmarkTrials.trial: BRCA1/BRCA2 Mutation Carriers — Platinum-Based Chemotherapy and PARP Inhibitors (2019, NEJM — POLO)
landmarkTrials.population: 154 patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer and germline BRCA1/2 mutations who had not progressed on first-line platinum
landmarkTrials.outcome: Olaparib: median PFS 7.4 vs 3.8 months (p=0.004); OS trend but not significant (19.0 vs 19.2 months). Confirmed benefit of targeted therapy in biomarker-selected patients. PARP inhibitors effective in BRCA-mutated pancreatic cancer.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: BRCA testing rarely available in Kenya; Olaparib not accessible; platinum agents (cisplatin/carboplatin) are available

evidenceTable.recommendation: Gem + Capecitabine as alternative adjuvant regimen
Upfront surgery acceptable; neoadjuvant for borderline
PARP inhibitor maintenance in BRCA carriers
Monitor CA 19-9 every 2-3 cycles of chemotherapy
Achieve R0 resection; re-resect positive margins if feasible

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical suspicion — painless jaundice + weight loss + epigastric/back pain + new-onset diabetes in an older patient; Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder); Trousseau sign (migratory thrombophlebitis) — rare but specific

Step 2: CT pancreas protocol (triple-phase) — arterial phase (25-35s) for arterial anatomy; pancreatic parenchymal phase (40-50s) for tumor detection; portal venous phase (60-70s) for venous involvement and liver metastases. Sensitivity 90-95% for tumors >2 cm. Key features: hypodense, poorly marginated mass; double duct sign

Step 3: Assess resectability on CT — resectable: no arterial involvement, ≤180° SMV/PV involvement, no distant mets; borderline resectable: gastroduodenal artery abutment, >180° SMV/PV, limited SMA abutment; locally advanced: >180° SMA/celiac, unreconstructable SMV/PV; metastatic: distant metastases

Step 4: CA 19-9 — baseline and serial monitoring; >100 U/mL suspicious; >1000 U/mL strongly suggests metastatic disease; false positive in cholangitis, biliary obstruction, pancreatitis; Lewis antigen negative patients (5-10%) cannot produce CA 19-9 → use CEA instead

Step 5: EUS + FNA for tissue diagnosis — indications: confirm diagnosis before neoadjuvant therapy, confirm borderline/unresectable before non-surgical therapy, cystic lesions, small tumors not visible on CT. FNA sensitivity 85-95%, specificity >95%. Risk of tumor seeding along needle tract (very low with FNA — 2%)

Step 6: MRCP/MRI — better for ductal anatomy (double duct sign), cystic lesions, small liver metastases not seen on CT; MRCP useful when ERCP not feasible; MRI with contrast for characterizing indeterminate liver lesions

Step 7: Diagnostic laparoscopy — indications: equivocal CT findings, high-risk patients (CA 19-9 >1000, large tumors, ascites), planned neoadjuvant therapy; detects peritoneal and superficial liver metastases missed by CT (10-20% of patients); should be done before committing to laparotomy

Step 8: Staging laparotomy — only for patients with confirmed resectable disease; abdominal exploration for peritoneal/liver metastases; Kocher maneuver to assess posterior involvement; assessment of SMA and SMV involvement; biopsy of suspicious nodes
Step 9: PET-CT — when CT findings are equivocal for metastases, high CA 19-9 with negative CT, or post-treatment assessment; not essential for routine staging but helpful in selected cases

Kenya adaptation: CT pancreas protocol available at tertiary centers; EUS + FNA at 2-3 centers; CA 19-9 widely available; diagnostic laparoscopy limited; many patients diagnosed at advanced stage; exploratory laparotomy with biopsy is still performed when advanced imaging is unavailable
managementPearls: Pancreatic cancer staging is mostly about resectability, not TNM stage: resectable (stage I-IIA), borderline resectable (IIA-IIB), locally advanced/unresectable (III), metastatic (IV). Treatment intent differs dramatically. Patients with stage IV disease should NOT undergo Whipple (no survival benefit, high morbidity)

CA 19-9 is the best biomarker but has limitations: biliary obstruction and cholangitis elevate CA 19-9 independent of tumor; check CA 19-9 after biliary drainage to get true baseline; Lewis a/b negative patients (5-10% of population, higher in some African populations) cannot synthesize CA 19-9 — use CEA for monitoring in these patients

The double duct sign (dilated CBD and pancreatic duct with a cutoff at the same level) on CT/MRCP is highly specific for a pancreatic head mass — when present, assume it is pancreatic cancer until proven otherwise. Even if no mass is visible, the double duct sign warrants further investigation

Pre-operative biliary drainage should be avoided in most patients with resectable pancreatic cancer: it increases complications (pancreatitis 5%, cholangitis 8%, stent occlusion, bleeding) without reducing post-surgical morbidity. Drain only if: bilirubin >15 mg/dL (250 µmol/L), cholangitis, severe pruritus, or prolonged wait for surgery (>2 weeks)

Endoscopic ultrasound is superior to CT for detecting small tumors (<2 cm) and for tissue acquisition; if CT shows a mass and the patient is going straight to surgery, FNA is not necessary. FNA is essential before neoadjuvant therapy (to confirm diagnosis), for unresectable disease, and to differentiate from other lesions (autoimmune pancreatitis, neuroendocrine tumors)

Pancreatic enzyme replacement: ALL patients with pancreatic cancer (especially after pancreaticoduodenectomy or with ductal obstruction) need pancreatic enzymes (Creon 25,000-50,000 U orally with each meal, 10,000-25,000 U with snacks). Do not prescribe enteric-coated pancreatic enzymes — they must be taken with food. Undertreated exocrine insufficiency is a major cause of malnutrition

Pain management should be aggressive and multimodal: WHO analgesic ladder (paracetamol → NSAIDs → opioids). Celiac plexus block/neurolysis should be considered early for treatment-resistant pain (improves pain control, reduces opioid use, may improve appetite). Do not wait until the patient is on high-dose opioids — refer early

Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis: pancreatic cancer has one of the highest risks of VTE among all cancers (20-30% incidence); give prophylactic LMWH/low-molecular-weight heparin during hospitalization; consider primary prophylaxis with LMWH as outpatient if high Khorana score or metastatic disease; DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) are alternatives for treatment

Cachexia management: pancreatic cancer cachexia is driven by inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), tumor-derived factors, and metabolic derangements; multimodal approach: nutritional counseling, pancreatic enzymes, appetite stimulants (megestrol acetate 160-480 mg/day), exercise program; early nutritionist referral is essential — do not wait until the patient is severely malnourished

New-onset diabetes in an elderly patient (>60 years) with weight loss and no family history of diabetes: suspect pancreatic cancer until proven otherwise. Up to 30% of pancreatic cancer patients have new-onset diabetes within 2 years of diagnosis. The diabetes is caused by tumor-secreted products (adrenomedullin) causing insulin resistance

In Kenyan practice, cost and availability significantly influence treatment: many patients cannot afford mFOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel; Gemcitabine monotherapy remains the most accessible option; access to Whipple surgery is limited; early palliative care referral is essential for advanced disease

KRAS testing is not yet part of routine practice in Kenya, but it is becoming increasingly relevant for targeted therapy trials and may guide treatment in the future; currently, the most important biomarker is CA 19-9 for monitoring and BRCA status (where platinum/PARP inhibitors may be beneficial)
operativeNuances: Whipple procedure patient positioning: supine, arms out; central venous line and arterial line; 2-4 units of cross-matched blood available; midline incision from xiphisternum to below umbilicus; midline approach allows extension if needed for vascular reconstruction

Kocher maneuver technique: lift duodenum and pancreatic head medially; dissect avascular plane between duodenum and IVC/aorta; mobilize duodenum and head of pancreas completely off the retroperitoneum — allows palpation of tumor and assessment of SMA and SMV involvement. Extend from foramen of Winslow to the ligament of Treitz

Assessment of resectability: determine relationship of tumor to (1) celiac axis and common hepatic artery, (2) SMA (the critical artery — tumor involvement >180° = unresectable), (3) SMV and portal vein confluence (≤180° = resectable with or without venous resection; >180° with occlusion = unresectable), (4) superior mesenteric artery in uncinate groove

Dividing the pancreatic neck: pass a blunt clamp (e.g., Lahey clamp) gently behind the pancreatic neck, anterior to the SMV-PV confluence; elevate the neck and divide using electrocautery or scalpel; watch for the 'venae comitantes' — the small veins running with the pancreatic duct that can cause bleeding; oversew the pancreatic stump on the body side

Pancreaticojejunostomy (the most critical anastomosis): end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy in two layers; inner layer: pancreatic duct-to-jejunal mucosa with 5-0 or 6-0 absorbable monofilament sutures (6-10 interrupted sutures); outer layer: pancreatic parenchyma-to-jejunal seromuscular with 3-0 silk; use a pancreatic duct stent (5-8 Fr pediatric feeding tube) to guide suturing. Duct-to-mucosa technique reduces leak rate vs dunking technique

Handling the 'soft pancreas with small duct' — highest risk for pancreatic leak: consider pancreaticogastrostomy (lower leak rate per meta-analysis), placement of a small (3 Fr) pancreatic duct stent, prophylactic octreotide (controversial, some evidence supports it in soft pancreas), drain near the anastomosis

Vascular resection (portal vein/SMV): when the tumor is adherent but not completely encasing the vein, a partial venous resection (4-5 cm) with primary end-to-end anastomosis or graft interposition (internal jugular vein or PTFE) is possible; the key principle: obtain negative margin (R0) even if it requires venous resection — survival is comparable to standard Whipple if venous resection is needed and successful

Distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy: approach lesser sac through gastrocolic ligament; mobilize splenic flexure of colon; ligate splenic artery and vein at the pancreatic tail; divide the pancreas with stapler (with or without reinforcement) or scalpel with oversewing of the pancreatic duct; drain at the resection margin; splenectomy increases infection risk — vaccinate for encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae type B) after surgery

In Kenyan setting where advanced surgical expertise may be limited, a stepwise approach: (1) confirm diagnosis with CT + FNA if available; (2) refer to a specialist center (Nairobi, Mombasa) for Whipple evaluation; (3) if Whipple not possible, palliative biliary stenting (endoscopic or PTC) for jaundice; (4) celiac plexus block for pain; (5) palliative chemotherapy if affordable; (6) best supportive care, including pancreatic enzymes, nutritional support, and pain management

prognosis.resectablePancreaticCancer: Median OS 24-30 months with surgery + adjuvant therapy; 5-year OS 20-30% (mFOLFIRINOX) or 15-20% (gem-based). R0 resection most important prognostic factor. Nodal status (node positive: 5-year OS 10-15% vs node negative: 30-40%), tumor size, differentiation, and CA 19-9 response also predictive.
prognosis.borderlineResectable: Median OS 18-22 months after neoadjuvant therapy + surgery; 30-50% undergo successful resection after neoadjuvant; R0 rate 80-90% with neoadjuvant vs 60-70% with upfront surgery; survival for those resected after neoadjuvant similar to resectable group.
prognosis.locallyAdvancedUnresectable: Median OS 12-16 months with chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX/Gem + Nab-paclitaxel); 5-year OS <5%; best outcomes with induction chemotherapy + possible chemoradiation; rare patients may be downstaged to resection (<10%)
prognosis.metastaticDisease: Median OS 6-11 months with chemotherapy; 1-year survival 30-35% (FOLFIRINOX) vs 20-25% (Gem + Nab-paclitaxel) vs 15-20% (Gem alone); 5-year OS <2%; PARP inhibitor maintenance for BRCA carriers
prognosticFactors: Resectability status at diagnosis — single most important prognostic factor
Margins (R0 vs R1) — R0 confers 2x survival advantage over R1
Lymph node status — involved nodes halve survival; node ratio >0.2 particularly poor
CA 19-9 level and response — >1000 U/mL at baseline or failure to decline with treatment predicts poor outcome
Tumor size >2 cm, poor differentiation, vascular invasion — adverse features
Performance status (ECOG) — ECOG 0-1 have best outcomes with chemotherapy
Treatment received — adjuvant chemotherapy essential for survival after resection
BRCA/PALB2 mutation carriers — better response to platinum, longer survival
Genetic signature — KRAS wild type has better prognosis than KRAS mutant
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Majority present with advanced unresectable disease (80-90% at diagnosis) due to late presentation; Whipple surgery is available at 2-3 centers but cost-prohibitive for many; adjuvant chemotherapy frequently not completed due to cost and toxicity; median survival in Kenyan practice is 3-6 months after diagnosis; palliative care and pain management are dominant treatment modalities

history_questions.question: Have you noticed yellowing of your eyes or skin?
Is the jaundice painless? Did it come on gradually?
Do you have pain in the upper abdomen that goes through to your back?
Is the pain better when you lean forward?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past 3-6 months?
Have you lost your appetite?
Have you been diagnosed with diabetes recently or has your diabetes become harder to control?
Do your stools float, smell foul, or look greasy (steatorrhea)?
Do you smoke cigarettes?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or bottled beer?
Has anyone in your family had pancreatic cancer?
Have you had chronic pancreatitis or recurrent pancreatitis?
Do you have dark urine and pale stools?
Do you feel nauseous or have you been vomiting?
Have you noticed a lump in your upper abdomen?
Are you over 60 years old?
Do you have a history of diabetes or obesity?
Have you had any blood clots or DVT recently?
Do you have itching all over your body?
Is the pain constant or does it come and go? Does it wake you at night?
Do you have difficulty swallowing or feel full quickly after eating?
Have you had any episodes of vomiting (food you ate hours ago)?
Have you noticed a change in your bowel habits — diarrhea, constipation, or floating stools?
Do you feel bloated or have a swollen abdomen?
Have you been told you have a blood clot (DVT) or had a pulmonary embolism?
Have you had any recent episodes of pancreatitis (inflammation of the pancreas)?
Have you lost more than 10% of your body weight in the last 6 months?
Is there a history of breast, ovarian, or pancreatic cancer in your family?
Do you have any known genetic condition such as Peutz-Jeghers or Lynch syndrome?
Do you eat a diet high in processed meats, smoked foods, or red meat?
Have you been exposed to pesticides or industrial chemicals in your work?
Do you have a history of chronic pancreatitis from any cause?
Are you over 60 years of age?
Do you have high triglycerides or a history of gallstones?
Have you had your gallbladder removed?
Do you have difficulty managing your diabetes (if applicable)?
Have you experienced any episodes of confusion or dizziness (possible hypoglycemia)?

examination_findings.finding: Jaundice — yellow sclera and generalized icterus
Courvoisier sign — palpable, non-tender gallbladder (pathognomonic for malignant distal CBD obstruction)
Epigastric mass — firm, irregular, fixed
Hepatomegaly from metastases
Ascites from peritoneal carcinomatosis
Cachexia — severe muscle wasting and loss of subcutaneous fat
Supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow's node — left)
Trousseau sign — migratory superficial thrombophlebitis (paraneoplastic)
Excoriations from pruritus (bile salt deposition)
Pallor from anemia of chronic disease
Abdominal distension from ascites or tumor mass
Splenomegaly if splenic vein thrombosis
Rectal Blumer's shelf (peritoneal seeding on DRE)
Right upper quadrant tenderness
Finger clubbing (rare paraneoplastic)
Palpable mid-abdominal mass — firm, irregular, fixed to underlying structures (late sign)
Left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow's node/Troisier's sign)
Periumbilical nodule (Sister Mary Joseph nodule) — metastatic implantation
Rectal shelf (Blumer's shelf) — peritoneal deposit felt on DRE in pouch of Douglas
Palpable left supraclavicular node (Virchow's node) — thoracic duct drainage of abdominal malignancy
Dilated abdominal wall veins (portal/splenic vein obstruction)
Splenomegaly from splenic vein thrombosis (body/tail tumors)
Signs of DVT — unilateral leg swelling, warmth, Homan's sign (Trousseau syndrome)
Fever if obstructive cholangitis or tumor necrosis
Overt signs of malnutrition — temporal wasting, loss of thenar eminence, severe cachexia
Palpable periumbilical lymph node (Sister Mary Joseph)
Left pleural effusion from subdiaphragmatic invasion or metastases
Scratch marks (excorations) from pruritus due to cholestasis
Signs of heparin injection sites if on VTE prophylaxis
Pale conjunctiva from anemia of chronic disease or GI blood loss
Muscle wasting of the thenar and hypothenar eminences — chronic disease

bedside_scores.name: ECOG Performance Status
bedside_scores.interpretation: ECOG 0-1: candidate for surgery/chemotherapy; ECOG 2+: consider palliative approach

riskFactors.note: Strongest modifiable risk factor — 2x risk in current smokers
Diabetes >1 year duration is risk; new-onset diabetes in elderly may be early sign
Blood group A/B/AB associated with modest increase

subtypes.name: Pancreatic Head Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.management: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) for resectable disease. Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for borderline resectable. Biliary stenting for palliation. Adjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX or Gemcitabine + Capecitabine).

subtypes.name: Pancreatic Body/Tail Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.management: Distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy for resectable disease. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy for borderline resectable/locally advanced. Palliative chemotherapy for metastatic disease.

subtypes.name: Unresectable/Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer
subtypes.management: Palliative chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX or Gemcitabine + Nab-paclitaxel). Biliary stenting for jaundice. Celiac plexus block/neurolysis for pain. Palliative gastrojejunostomy for duodenal obstruction. Hospice referral.

mimics.whyMimic: Painless jaundice, weight loss, obstructive LFTs
Epigastric pain, weight loss, pancreatic mass on imaging
Pancreatic mass on imaging, possible jaundice
Pancreatic mass, jaundice, abdominal pain, weight loss

mimics.diseaseName: Metastatic Tumor to Pancreas (RCC, Melanoma, Lung, Breast)
mimics.whyMimic: Pancreatic mass on imaging, possible obstruction
Pancreatic cystic lesion, may cause symptoms if large
Cystic pancreatic mass, may have solid components
Epigastric mass, pain, may be pulsatile
Pancreatic mass, weight loss, abdominal pain, fever

laboratory.name: CA 19-9
laboratory.indication: Best available tumor marker; >100 U/mL suspicious, >1000 usually metastatic

laboratory.name: Bilirubin (total + direct), ALP, GGT, ALT, AST
Albumin
laboratory.indication: Nutritional status — low albumin predicts poor outcome

laboratory.name: INR / Coagulation profile
laboratory.indication: Vitamin K malabsorption from obstructive jaundice

laboratory.name: Random blood glucose / HbA1c
laboratory.indication: New onset diabetes or worsening glucose control

imaging.name: CT Pancreas Protocol (triple-phase / pancreatic phase)
imaging.indication: Best for staging and resectability — assess vascular involvement (SMA, SMV, celiac, portal vein)

imaging.name: MRCP / MRI Abdomen
imaging.indication: Better for ductal anatomy, liver lesions, double duct sign

imaging.name: Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS) + Fine Needle Aspiration (FNA)
imaging.indication: Tissue diagnosis and local staging; FNA for cytology

imaging.name: PET-CT
imaging.indication: Detect occult metastases, confirm staging before surgery

imaging.name: Diagnostic Laparoscopy
imaging.indication: Detect peritoneal/liver surface metastases before committing to laparotomy

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.indication: Therapeutic — biliary stent placement for jaundice

resuscitation: Assess hydration and correct with IV fluids if dehydrated
Correct coagulopathy: Vitamin K 10 mg IV daily ×3 days
Pain control: WHO ladder. For severe pain: consider celiac plexus block early.
Nutritional support: oral pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon 25,000-50,000 U with meals)
biliary_drainage: If bilirubin >15 or cholangitis: pre-operative biliary drainage via ERCP + metal stent
Pre-op drainage debated — only for bilirubin >15, cholangitis, or delayed surgery

surgical.indication: Resectable disease (no arterial involvement, SMV/PV involvement <180 degrees, no metastases)
surgical.whipple: Standard pancreaticoduodenectomy (head tumors): en-bloc removal of pancreatic head, duodenum, distal CBD, gallbladder, distal stomach (antrectomy), and regional lymph nodes
surgical.distal: Distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy for body/tail tumors
surgical.approach: Open or laparoscopic (selected centers); vascular reconstruction if needed

nonOperative: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX: for borderline resectable — 4-6 cycles then restaging
Adjuvant chemotherapy: mFOLFIRINOX (preferred) or Gemcitabine + Capecitabine for 6 months
Palliative chemotherapy: FOLFIRINOX, Gemcitabine + Nab-paclitaxel for metastatic
Palliative radiotherapy: for pain control in locally advanced

general: NG tube on free drainage — remove day 3-5
Drains: monitor output and amylase (pancreatic leak)
IV fluids with glucose monitoring — check blood glucose 4-hourly
Octreotide 100-150 mcg SC 8-hourly to reduce pancreatic secretions (controversial)
Nutrition: consider NJ tube feeding or TPN if prolonged NBM
pancreatic_anastomosis_monitoring: Drain amylase on day 1, 3, 5 — >3x serum = pancreatic leak
If drain amylase elevated: keep drain, NBM, consider TPN/ NJ feeds
CT if fever or concern for collection
diet_advancement: Day 3-5: Clear fluids if NG removed and no leak
Lifelong: pancreatic enzyme replacement with meals, small frequent meals
discharge_criteria: No evidence of pancreatic leak (drain amylase normal or drain removed)
Histology and staging confirmed, adjuvant therapy plan made

pathways.criteria: No arterial involvement, ≤180 degree SMV/PV involvement, no metastases
pathways.treatment: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) for head tumors. Distal pancreatectomy for body/tail. Adjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX or Gemcitabine + Capecitabine).

pathways.criteria: >180 degree SMV/PV involvement, limited arterial abutment, no metastases
pathways.treatment: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for 2-6 months. Restaging CT. Pancreaticoduodenectomy with vascular resection if downstaged. Adjuvant therapy.

pathways.criteria: Distant metastases, arterial invasion, extensive SMV involvement
pathways.treatment: Palliative chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX or Gemcitabine + Nab-paclitaxel). Biliary stenting. Celiac plexus block for pain. Palliative gastrojejunostomy for duodenal obstruction. Hospice.

steps: Supine position, midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
Exploratory laparotomy — exclude peritoneal/liver metastases (if present, abort)
Kocher maneuver — mobilize duodenum and pancreatic head off IVC and aorta
Assess SMA and SMV involvement — determine resectability
Open lesser sac — divide gastrocolic ligament
Divide gastroduodenal artery (test clamp — confirm hepatic artery flow)
Divide duodenum distal to pylorus (pylorus-preserving) or antrectomy (classic Whipple)
Divide jejunum at ligament of Treitz, pass retromesenteric
Divide SMV and portal vein branches to uncinate process
Remove specimen (head, neck, uncinate, duodenum, CBD, GB)
Place drains near pancreaticojejunostomy and hepaticojejunostomy
operative_templates.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis

dischargeCriteria: Drain amylase normal or drain removed (no pancreatic leak)
Histology and staging discussed, adjuvant therapy plan made

patient_education.explanation: Pancreatic cancer is a malignant tumor of the pancreas — an organ behind your stomach that produces digestive juices and hormones (including insulin). It often causes jaundice (yellow skin), weight loss, and pain in the upper back. It is a serious cancer. Surgery (Whipple procedure) offers the best chance of cure for early-stage disease. Chemotherapy is used before and/or after surgery.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if: jaundice worsens, you develop fever with chills, you have severe abdominal or back pain, you vomit blood or pass black stools, you experience severe nausea and vomiting, or your wound becomes red, swollen, or discharges pus.
patient_education.activity: After surgery: start walking day 1. Increase activity gradually. Avoid heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks after Whipple surgery. You will tire easily during chemotherapy. Plan rest periods.
patient_education.diet: Eat small, frequent meals (6-8 per day). Take pancreatic enzyme capsules (Creon) with every meal and snack — they help digest food and prevent weight loss. Avoid large meals. Avoid high-fiber foods if you have diarrhea. Drink plenty of water. Good foods: soft rice, porridge, mashed potatoes, eggs, fish, chicken, bananas. Avoid: greasy/fried foods, alcohol.
patient_education.medication: Take pancreatic enzymes (Creon) with all meals and snacks. If diabetic: monitor blood sugar and take insulin as prescribed. Take pain medication as prescribed — do not wait for severe pain. Consider celiac plexus block for persistent pain.
patient_education.followUp: Surgical clinic at 2 weeks: wound check, histology results. Oncology clinic at 4-6 weeks: start adjuvant chemotherapy. CT scan every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months. CA 19-9 monitoring with each visit. Nutritionist and diabetic educator review.

before_discharge: Histology, stage, and treatment plan discussed with patient and family
Pancreatic enzyme replacement prescribed and explained
Wound care and drain care teaching

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic — wound check, histology results, drain removal if still in
Oncology review — start adjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX preferred)
CT scan, CA 19-9, nutritional assessment, diabetes control
Annual surveillance: CT, CA 19-9, clinical review

when_to_return: Severe back pain not controlled with medication
Inability to eat or keep food down
